<title>Cradle-to-factory gate environmental impact of the dairy sector in Haryana, India </title>
R. Kumar Gautam E. Rani1 A. Malik

Received: 9 September 2023 / Revised: 19 February 2024 / Accepted: 14 May 2024 © The Author(s) under exclusive licence to Iranian Society of Environmentalists (IRSEN) and Science and Research Branch, Islamic Azad University 2024
1: <header>Abstract </header>

A significant amount of GHG is produced during milk production and dairy product processing and manufacturing. In this context, the current research was carried out in Haryana (India), with the objective to estimate the GHGs emission from dairy buffaloes and dairy products. Further, by using LCA methodology and tier-2 technique of IPCC guidelines, this research estimated the CF (Carbon Footprint) of raw milk production from buffalo using the "cradle to farm gate" system boundary and CF of major Indian dairy products using "Farm gate to factory gate" system boundary. LCA calculations were as per ISO: 14,040 and 14,044 to detect and measure the effect within system boundary. The result showed that the average CF for raw milk production was 2.24 kg CO2-eq/ Kg FPCM. The study estimated the most significant source for GHG (tonne CO2-eq. head year-1 contribution was from enteric fermentation (3.27), followed by feed and fodder production (1.37) and manure-management (0.29). The CF of Ghee, curd, and Pasteurized milk were 4.2, 1.8 and 0.24 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The overall CF of dairy products (Kg CO2-eq) was significantly higher for Ghee (47.6), followed by Curd (4.28) and pasteur- ized milk (3.68). The emission values for GHGs in the study were estimated on higher side as compared to other countries due to low productivity, poor management practices, higher emission factors and poor environmental efficiency of the dairy sector. Further studies to accurately estimate CF of milk from specialized farms using LCA while giving a special concern to multifunctionality of animals are advocated.
Keywords Milk and dairy products Life cycle assessment . Carbon footprint

3: <header>Introduction </header>

Recent global "Go Green" initiatives have increased atten- tion to the environmental effects of the food production system among civil society, the relevant governments, and individual consumers. Globally, food consumption is among the most resource-intensive activities and one of the primary causes of environmental consequences (Notarnicola et al. 2017), accounting for between 21 and 37% of all GHG emis- sions (IPCC 2019). The food sector has transformed over the past ten years as a result of the environmental impact of population expansion on factors like biodiversity loss,
Editorial responsibility: S. Mirkia.
E. Rani ektamahi103@gmail.com 1 Department of Veterinary and Animal Husbandry Extension Education, Lala Lajpat Rai University of Veterinary and Animal Sciences, Hisar, Haryana 125004, India

climate change, water quality, soil quality, and many more (Kumar and Choubey 2021). According to van Dooren et al. (2014), current patterns in food production and consumption are deemed unsustainable, and around one-third of human impact on climate change and land usage is due to dietary preferences and the food supply chain (Vringer et al. 2010; Garnett 2011). Poore and Nemecek (2018) calculated that food contributes to 26% of total GHG emissions, with ani- mal foods having a greater CF than plant-based diets, to help break down which foods are the major contributors. Gerber et al. (2013) stated that 7100 Tg carbon dioxide per year was emitted from livestock (direct and indirect) sector and 5656 Tg carbon dioxide per year was emitted from the global transportation sector.

According to studies, the production of dairy products produced the second-highest amount of GHGs after that meat, accounting for around 4% of all GHGs, 10% of the world's capacity for eutrophication, and 6% of potential for acidification (Rufí-Salís, et al. 2020). This demonstrated that the dairy industry was resource-intensive, resulting in

significant energy, water, chemical use, etc. that had a signif- icant environmental impact (Mahath et al. 2019). Therefore, it is crucial for this sector to focus on achieving sustainable expansion. As stated by Konieczny and Kijowski (2005), sustainability necessitates the attempt of obtaining raw mate- rials from animal and plant origins, processing them, and distributing finished food products while also systematically identifying and monitoring important environmental aspects along the whole food chain (Ai et al. 2020). CF which is described as "a measure of the total CO2 and other gas emissions released by a specific product over its life cycle" (Zhong et al. 2019), is essential when taking sustainability factors like user waste output, energy consumption, global warming, developments in the production of food products, and recycling procedures into account (Rondoni and Grasso 2021).

The dairy sector includes dairy production and process- ing, which are essential to country economies and people's livelihoods comprised 133 million dairy farms across the globe and 9% of the total world's milk production is traded every year (IDF, 2022). This sector has grown rapidly at a mean growth rate of 1.8% per year and projected as it would be double in 2050 that of 2020 (Uctug 2019) due to a large rise in demand in emerging countries driven by an increase in population, money, and lifestyle changes (FAO 2022). Milk being an important source of nutrition has resulted in an 18% rise in GHG emissions (FAO and GDP 2018). Each year around 600 million tonnes of CH4 is released in atmos- phere. Out of this, 60% are sourced from anthropogenic activities. Globally, the total anthropogenic GHG emissions by economic sectors for the year 2019 was reported to be 59 Gt. CO2-eq. Industry sector was the highest emitter (24%), followed by Electricity and Heat sector (23%), Agriculture, Forestry and Other Land Use (AFOLU) (22%), and transpor- tation (15%) stated by IPCC (2022). Agriculture (livestock farming and crop cultivation) and deforestation are the most common activities responsible for majority of GHG emis- sions from AFOLU sector. Almost world's half of the CH4 from human activities is being emitted by the agriculture sector alone, followed by fossil fuel and waste management. Enteric fermentation and manure management activities are the main source of CH4 emission from livestock sector, mak- ing this sector one of the largest GHG contributor (ibid). It has been reported that livestock contributes 14.5% of total global anthropogenic emissions. The primary GHG emis- sions from livestock are CO2, CH4 and N20. CH4 contributes the highest to GHG emissions from human activities (44%), followed by N2O (29%) and CO2 (27%). Gerber et al. (2013) reported the role of beef cattle in GHG emission, they stated that the beef cattle are the major contributor with 41% of the total livestock sector's emission, followed by dairy cattle (20%), pig sector (9%), poultry (8%) buffalo (8%) and small ruminant.

India, the largest milk producer in the world, is expected to contribute more than 50% of the world's milk output dur- ing the next ten years and more than 30% by 2030 (FAO, 2021) recognized as 'the oyster' of the global dairy indus- try with 20.17% share in total production of milk within the world (APEDA 2018). Dairy products were inextrica- bly linked to EI; hence this would result in a rise in GHG emissions (FAO, 2019). (Rotz et al. 2021; Chalermthai et al. 2021). As per the "Department of Animal Husbandry and Dairying GOI", the per capita availability of milk is 427 g/ day and the countries' milk production has shown growth of 5.29% over 2021, making a total production count of 221.06 MMT for the year 2022 (DAHD 2023).

There are several elements that impact the dairy sector's sustainability, and knowing the emission sources on dairy farms enables the development of new strategies to mini- mize emissions. This procedure starts with LCA. LCA is the most effective technique for identifying environmental changes caused by the manufacturing and use of a product and their possible influence on human existence (IDF 2015; Rufí-Salís et al. 2020). It tests different damage categories (Ecosystem, Human health and Resource depletion) by assessing numerous midpoint effect categories by analys- ing the product from raw material through recycling or final disposal of the product. Milk production, packaging, and processing have all been studied using LCAs as a technique for a comprehensive evaluation of the "environmental sus- tainability" of products by taking into account all life cycle phases (Hospido et al. 2003; Keoleian et al. 2004; Mattsson and Cederberg, 2000; Thomassen et al. 2008; Gerber et al. 2010; Heller & Keoleian 2011). Different environmental effects are investigated under various scenarios, but carbon footprint (GHG emission) assessed as equivalent CO2 per kg of product is the main researched impact category. The Indian livestock sector emitted 14.53 Mt CH4 and 0.178 Mt N2O and total GHG emissions in terms of CO2-eq was 243.128 Mt for the year 2015 (Dhingra et al. 2019).

The dairy sector has established environmental steward- ship objectives for 2050, including being carbon neutral or better, promoting recycling while minimizing water usage, and enhancing water quality by making the best use of nutri- ents and manure (Rotz et al. 2021). Greenhouse gas emissions can vary with different milk production systems, practices, and site conditions and their contributions need to be fully accounted for using consistent methodologies. Therefore, everyone's capacity to generally enhance the environmental performance of the dairy production system is increased by increasing understanding of the effects of production alter- natives and the supply chain. Many studies have been done to assess the CF of milk from cradle to farm gate bounda- ries of Indian dairy farms but very few analyses of CF were performed from farm gate to factory gate (Transportation, processing, packaging and cleaning). The work from farm

gate to factory gate includes transportation of milk from farm to dairy plant, different processing steps involved in products manufacturing, different packaging machines and material and cleaning of dairy plant equipment's. We decided to esti- mate the CF of pasteurized milk, Ghee and curd at one of the dairy plants in Haryana due to the lack of reliable data. Milk, ghee, and curd are focused in this investigation as these are the main dairy products of the Indian dairy sector.

(2013) reported the role of beef cattle in GHG emission, they stated that the beef cattle are the major contributor with 41% of the total livestock sector's emission, followed by dairy cattle (20%), pig sector (9%), poultry (8%) buffalo (8%) and small ruminant.

India, the largest milk producer in the world, is expected to contribute more than 50% of the world's milk output dur- ing the next ten years and more than 30% by 2030 (FAO, 2021) recognized as 'the oyster' of the global dairy indus- try with 20.17% share in total production of milk within the world (APEDA 2018). Dairy products were inextrica- bly linked to EI; hence this would result in a rise in GHG emissions (FAO, 2019). (Rotz et al. 2021; Chalermthai et al. 2021). As per the "Department of Animal Husbandry and Dairying GOI", the per capita availability of milk is 427 g/ day and the countries' milk production has shown growth of 5.29% over 2021, making a total production count of 221.06 MMT for the year 2022 (DAHD 2023).

There are several elements that impact the dairy sector's sustainability, and knowing the emission sources on dairy farms enables the development of new strategies to mini- mize emissions. This procedure starts with LCA. LCA is the most effective technique for identifying environmental changes caused by the manufacturing and use of a product and their possible influence on human existence (IDF 2015; Rufí-Salís et al. 2020). It tests different damage categories (Ecosystem, Human health and Resource depletion) by assessing numerous midpoint effect categories by analys- ing the product from raw material through recycling or final disposal of the product. Milk production, packaging, and processing have all been studied using LCAs as a technique for a comprehensive evaluation of the "environmental sus- tainability" of products by taking into account all life cycle phases (Hospido et al. 2003; Keoleian et al. 2004; Mattsson and Cederberg, 2000; Thomassen et al. 2008; Gerber et al. 2010; Heller & Keoleian 2011). Different environmental effects are investigated under various scenarios, but carbon footprint (GHG emission) assessed as equivalent CO2 per kg of product is the main researched impact category. The Indian livestock sector emitted 14.53 Mt CH4 and 0.178 Mt N2O and total GHG emissions in terms of CO2-eq was 243.128 Mt for the year 2015 (Dhingra et al. 2019).

The dairy sector has established environmental steward- ship objectives for 2050, including being carbon neutral or better, promoting recycling while minimizing water usage, and enhancing water quality by making the best use of nutri- ents and manure (Rotz et al. 2021). Greenhouse gas emissions can vary with different milk production systems, practices, and site conditions and their contributions need to be fully accounted for using consistent methodologies. Therefore, everyone's capacity to generally enhance the environmental performance of the dairy production system is increased by increasing understanding of the effects of production alter- natives and the supply chain. Many studies have been done to assess the CF of milk from cradle to farm gate bounda- ries of Indian dairy farms but very few analyses of CF were performed from farm gate to factory gate (Transportation, processing, packaging and cleaning). The work from farm

gate to factory gate includes transportation of milk from farm to dairy plant, different processing steps involved in products manufacturing, different packaging machines and material and cleaning of dairy plant equipment's. We decided to esti- mate the CF of pasteurized milk, Ghee and curd at one of the dairy plants in Haryana due to the lack of reliable data. Milk, ghee, and curd are focused in this investigation as these are the main dairy products of the Indian dairy sector.
7: <header>Introduction </header>

Recent global "Go Green" initiatives have increased atten- tion to the environmental effects of the food production system among civil society, the relevant governments, and individual consumers. Globally, food consumption is among the most resource-intensive activities and one of the primary causes of environmental consequences (Notarnicola et al. 2017), accounting for between 21 and 37% of all GHG emis- sions (IPCC 2019). The food sector has transformed over the past ten years as a result of the environmental impact of population expansion on factors like biodiversity loss,
Editorial responsibility: S. Mirkia.
E. Rani ektamahi103@gmail.com 1 Department of Veterinary and Animal Husbandry Extension Education, Lala Lajpat Rai University of Veterinary and Animal Sciences, Hisar, Haryana 125004, India

climate change, water quality, soil quality, and many more (Kumar and Choubey 2021). According to van Dooren et al. (2014), current patterns in food production and consumption are deemed unsustainable, and around one-third of human impact on climate change and land usage is due to dietary preferences and the food supply chain (Vringer et al. 2010; Garnett 2011). Poore and Nemecek (2018) calculated that food contributes to 26% of total GHG emissions, with ani- mal foods having a greater CF than plant-based diets, to help break down which foods are the major contributors. Gerber et al. (2013) stated that 7100 Tg carbon dioxide per year was emitted from livestock (direct and indirect) sector and 5656 Tg carbon dioxide per year was emitted from the global transportation sector.

According to studies, the production of dairy products produced the second-highest amount of GHGs after that meat, accounting for around 4% of all GHGs, 10% of the world's capacity for eutrophication, and 6% of potential for acidification (Rufí-Salís, et al. 2020). This demonstrated that the dairy industry was resource-intensive, resulting in

significant energy, water, chemical use, etc. that had a signif- icant environmental impact (Mahath et al. 2019). Therefore, it is crucial for this sector to focus on achieving sustainable expansion. As stated by Konieczny and Kijowski (2005), sustainability necessitates the attempt of obtaining raw mate- rials from animal and plant origins, processing them, and distributing finished food products while also systematically identifying and monitoring important environmental aspects along the whole food chain (Ai et al. 2020). CF which is described as "a measure of the total CO2 and other gas emissions released by a specific product over its life cycle" (Zhong et al. 2019), is essential when taking sustainability factors like user waste output, energy consumption, global warming, developments in the production of food products, and recycling procedures into account (Rondoni and Grasso 2021).

The dairy sector includes dairy production and process- ing, which are essential to country economies and people's livelihoods comprised 133 million dairy farms across the globe and 9% of the total world's milk production is traded every year (IDF, 2022). This sector has grown rapidly at a mean growth rate of 1.8% per year and projected as it would be double in 2050 that of 2020 (Uctug 2019) due to a large rise in demand in emerging countries driven by an increase in population, money, and lifestyle changes (FAO 2022). Milk being an important source of nutrition has resulted in an 18% rise in GHG emissions (FAO and GDP 2018). Each year around 600 million tonnes of CH4 is released in atmos- phere. Out of this, 60% are sourced from anthropogenic activities. Globally, the total anthropogenic GHG emissions by economic sectors for the year 2019 was reported to be 59 Gt. CO2-eq. Industry sector was the highest emitter (24%), followed by Electricity and Heat sector (23%), Agriculture, Forestry and Other Land Use (AFOLU) (22%), and transpor- tation (15%) stated by IPCC (2022). Agriculture (livestock farming and crop cultivation) and deforestation are the most common activities responsible for majority of GHG emis- sions from AFOLU sector. Almost world's half of the CH4 from human activities is being emitted by the agriculture sector alone, followed by fossil fuel and waste management. Enteric fermentation and manure management activities are the main source of CH4 emission from livestock sector, mak- ing this sector one of the largest GHG contributor (ibid). It has been reported that livestock contributes 14.5% of total global anthropogenic emissions. The primary GHG emis- sions from livestock are CO2, CH4 and N20. CH4 contributes the highest to GHG emissions from human activities (44%), followed by N2O (29%) and CO2 (27%). Gerber et al. (2013) reported the role of beef cattle in GHG emission, they stated that the beef cattle are the major contributor with 41% of the total livestock sector's emission, followed by dairy cattle (20%), pig sector (9%), poultry (8%) buffalo (8%) and small ruminant.

There are several elements that impact the dairy sector's sustainability, and knowing the emission sources on dairy farms enables the development of new strategies to mini- mize emissions. This procedure starts with LCA. LCA is the most effective technique for identifying environmental changes caused by the manufacturing and use of a product and their possible influence on human existence (IDF 2015; Rufí-Salís et al. 2020). It tests different damage categories (Ecosystem, Human health and Resource depletion) by assessing numerous midpoint effect categories by analys- ing the product from raw material through recycling or final disposal of the product. Milk production, packaging, and processing have all been studied using LCAs as a technique for a comprehensive evaluation of the "environmental sus- tainability" of products by taking into account all life cycle phases (Hospido et al. 2003; Keoleian et al. 2004; Mattsson and Cederberg, 2000; Thomassen et al. 2008; Gerber et al. 2010; Heller & Keoleian 2011). Different environmental effects are investigated under various scenarios, but carbon footprint (GHG emission) assessed as equivalent CO2 per kg of product is the main researched impact category. The Indian livestock sector emitted 14.53 Mt CH4 and 0.178 Mt N2O and total GHG emissions in terms of CO2-eq was 243.128 Mt for the year 2015 (Dhingra et al. 2019).

The dairy sector has established environmental steward- ship objectives for 2050, including being carbon neutral or better, promoting recycling while minimizing water usage, and enhancing water quality by making the best use of nutri- ents and manure (Rotz et al. 2021). Greenhouse gas emissions can vary with different milk production systems, practices, and site conditions and their contributions need to be fully accounted for using consistent methodologies. Therefore, everyone's capacity to generally enhance the environmental performance of the dairy production system is increased by increasing understanding of the effects of production alter- natives and the supply chain. Many studies have been done to assess the CF of milk from cradle to farm gate bounda- ries of Indian dairy farms but very few analyses of CF were performed from farm gate to factory gate (Transportation, processing, packaging and cleaning). The work from farm

gate to factory gate includes transportation of milk from farm to dairy plant, different processing steps involved in products manufacturing, different packaging machines and material and cleaning of dairy plant equipment's. We decided to esti- mate the CF of pasteurized milk, Ghee and curd at one of the dairy plants in Haryana due to the lack of reliable data. Milk, ghee, and curd are focused in this investigation as these are the main dairy products of the Indian dairy sector.
11: <header>Materials and methods </header>

Several indicators and techniques were established for evalu- ating and standardizing the environmental effects of diverse system instabilities. One of these is the well-known LCA process, which assesses a product's environmental effect over the course of its full life cycle (Lindeijer and Guinée, 2002). The LCA concept first emerged in the 1960s, and efforts to establish the LCA technique began in the 1970s. Since the 1990s, it has attracted interest in the field of environmental science. Since it provides a neutral way to characterize the production system and evaluate consequences in terms of outputs, the LCA approach is ideally suitable for analyzing agricultural systems (Casey and Holden 2005). Through- out this investigation, the ISO 14040 (ISO 2006a) and ISO 14044 LCA (ISO 2006b) standards were followed. LCA is the process of evaluating a product's potential environmental effect depending on a quantitative examina- tion of the product throughout its entire life cycle, detect- ing all environmental materials and energy emissions, and looking for chances to enhance the product's environmental performance (Huysveld et al. 2015). Goal and scope defini- tion, LCI ("Life Cycle Inventory") analysis, LCIA ("Life Cycle Impact Assessment"), and Interpretation, as specified by ISO are the four processes that often follow a detailed LCA (AzariJafari et al. 2016). The LCIA technique in the present work is simpler than the traditional LCA since it primarily employs several types of indicators to develop LCI data (Nigri et al. 2014). Nevertheless, only the product CF is taken into account for calculating the GWP: "Global Warm- ing Potential" in the impact category, which is expressed by Kg CO2-eq per Kg emission. In this research, additional effects like eutrophication, the potential for acid rain, toxic- ity, and SO on are left out. Since a lifecycle analysis might not necessarily use effect analysis, the LCI findings offer data on a product system, such as all outputs and inputs in the type of elementary flows (Seppälä, 2003), which is used in this study to determine the impact of carbon emissions. The LCA method is implemented in five steps: 1. Mapping the process; 2. Setting scope and boundaries; 3. Collecting data; 4. Calculating data; and 5. Evaluating and reporting (IDF 2015). The LCA's detailed framework is depicted in Fig. 1 below. The goal of LCA is to determine a product's

overall environmental effect while limiting emissions by determining the factors that have the greatest influence across a product's lifecycle and comparing emissions from two or more identical products and processes (Roy et al. 2009).

13: The attributional technique was used in this work to esti- mate the CF. The estimated FU ("Functional Unit") in the study was 1 kg of FPCM ("Fat- and Protein-Corrected Milk") at the farm gate. Milk typically contains 40 cal, of which 4% are from fat and 3% are from actual protein. In the post- firm LCA study, 1 kg of the final product (FU) was utilized for ghee (clarified butter) and curd, while 1 kg of packaged ECM ("Energy-Corrected Milk") was pasteurized milk exit- ing the plant. Other relevant dairy research (Mattsson and Cederberg, 2000; Holden and Casey, 2005; Flysjö et al. 2011; Bartl et al. 2011; González-Garca et al., 2018) supported the choice of this FU. The following equation, recommended by IDF (2015), was used to determine the FPCM:
FPCM (Kg/yr.) = Production (Kg/yr.) [0.1226 X Fat% + 0.0776 X True Protein% + 0.2534] (1)
Fig. 1 Steps for conducting LCA (Source: IDF 2015)

From the receipts issued to farmers by "dairy cooperative societies", milk sellers, and private dairy plants following the sale of milk, the daily milk production yield of the ani- mals was calculated. The Gerber and macro Kjeldahl meth- ods were used at the university lab to analyse milk samples taken from breastfeeding buffaloes on a monthly basis for fat and real protein levels.
14: The attributional technique was used in this work to esti- mate the CF. The estimated FU ("Functional Unit") in the study was 1 kg of FPCM ("Fat- and Protein-Corrected Milk") at the farm gate. Milk typically contains 40 cal, of which 4% are from fat and 3% are from actual protein. In the post- firm LCA study, 1 kg of the final product (FU) was utilized for ghee (clarified butter) and curd, while 1 kg of packaged ECM ("Energy-Corrected Milk") was pasteurized milk exit- ing the plant. Other relevant dairy research (Mattsson and Cederberg, 2000; Holden and Casey, 2005; Flysjö et al. 2011; Bartl et al. 2011; González-Garca et al., 2018) supported the choice of this FU. The following equation, recommended by IDF (2015), was used to determine the FPCM:
FPCM (Kg/yr.) = Production (Kg/yr.) [0.1226 X Fat% + 0.0776 X True Protein% + 0.2534] (1)
Fig. 1 Steps for conducting LCA (Source: IDF 2015)

From the receipts issued to farmers by "dairy cooperative societies", milk sellers, and private dairy plants following the sale of milk, the daily milk production yield of the ani- mals was calculated. The Gerber and macro Kjeldahl meth- ods were used at the university lab to analyse milk samples taken from breastfeeding buffaloes on a monthly basis for fat and real protein levels.
15: <header>2. Setting scope </header>

The system boundary, which plays a significant role in LCA, has a significant impact on the evaluation's accuracy (Park et al. 2016). Input-Output process diagrams are typically used in LCA to indicate system boundaries. The analysis used the "Cradle to Farm Gate" limit to calculate the CF of raw buf- falo milk production. All upstream information, including milk production, manure management, grazing, farming, animal pro- duction, etc., was taken into account in the cradle-to-farm gate approach. Additionally, CO2 emissions from livestock respira- tion and CO2 sequestration by crops have not been examined since they are thought to have a neutral impact on the potential for global warming (Bartl et al. 2011). The study including GHG emissions from Buffalo raw milk production with system boundary "Cradle to farm gate" is represented in Fig. 2.

For post-farm gate analysis of CF of ghee, curd, and milk pasteurization, the system boundary "Farm gate to Factory gate" was selected. For this, detailed information about all the processes incorporated in milk and milk product processing, the packaging material quantity and their respective material
compositions (PET, LDPE, cardboard, paper, etc.), electricity and fuel consumption, and transportation was collected (Fig. 3).

Allocation is one LCA issue that needs attention. The allocation typically enables the display of the environmen- tal burden connected to the several co-product produc- tions. It explains the division of "inputs" and "outputs" between the desired product and by-products. However, ISO-14044 (2006b) suggests avoiding allocation if practi- cal. Additionally, no allocation technique was used in this investigation.

There are a number of data gathering standards provided in ISO 14040-2006, however, the IDF ("International Dairy Federation)" releases rules for data collection modules, and a questionnaire style was chosen for the dairy business. All input-output and emission-related data were gathered and normalized according to functional units during this phase. In-depth data for "cradle to farm gate" was gathered during the interview on the home, agri- cultural farm size, buffalo farms herd composition, kinds and quantities of fodder cultivation, feeding and management, waste management, and milk production. A structured questionnaire was created to improve the data's precision and accuracy based on the IDF's published recommendations (IDF 2015). This dairy factory uses raw material as local raw milk of cattle and buffalo. Dairy fac- tories were asked to give statistics for the years 2022-2023 on a 'whole of factory bases. Data was gathered on-site between December 2022 and March 2023. The resources that were used at the dairy factory level were recorded. The acquired information was examined with the relevant staff to ensure that the data's metric units were consistent.

By utilizing the data gathered, the CF was quantified. The CF of the buffalo raw milk and dairy products was calculated by estimating all the GHG emissions from sev- eral sources, converting them into kilograms of Co2-eq, and adding them all together (expressed in CO2-eq). The following datasets and recommendations were used to cre- ate the tier-2 emission factors that were used in the study:

<list>(a) IPCC-2019, Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories. 
(b) (b Emission factors for feed components from Ecoin- vent v.2 (Ecoinvent, 2010).</list>

The GWP values of several GHGs proposed in IPCC (2014) are used in this study. One metric that includes all GHGs is CO2-eq. 0: <title>Results and discussion </title>
Production of milk
The farm-production system

The estimation was made using data from Haryana's small- holder buffalo farms and an appropriate sampling technique. The most significant output projections for smallholder buf- falo farms are included in Table 2. Male respondents were specifically chosen because they were known to make deci- sions on "animal husbandry" in the research area among family members.
1: <header>Greenhouse gas emission </header>

Among the most vital elements affecting the GHG emissions from smallholder farm production was the diet of the buf- falo. Table 3 summarizes the quantity of different feed and fodders fed to animals on a Dry Matter (DM) basis in a day.
Table 3 Estimated average dry matter Intake of buffalo

Sr. No.|Category of feed|Ingredients of feed|Kg. day-1 (Mean + SD) 1|Concentrate|Wheat bran|1.61 + 1.2 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed|0.84 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Wheat flour|0.22 + 0.3 1|Concentrate|Pearl millet grain|0.23 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Gram hull|0.31 + 0.6 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed cake|2.32 + 0.7 2|Dry fodder|Wheat straw|4.72 + 1.6 2|Dry fodder|Paddy straw|0.31 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Maize|0.20 + 0.6 3|Green fodder|Local grass|0.77 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Oat|0.42 + 0.5 3|Green fodder|Sorghum|2.32 + 1.1 3|Green fodder|Barseem|0.62 + 0.3 Total|||14.89

The data about feeds and fodders were converted into DM for each feed ingredient by applying DM weight issues sug- gested by Ranjhan, (1991) and Banerjee, (2012) for Indian feed and fodder. The animals in the stalls were only pro- vided with concentrates, agricultural byproducts, and a small amount of green grass to eat. The animals received varying amounts of green forage throughout the seasons. The main consumers of the more expensive feed, such as concentrates, were lactating buffalo. The average green fodder, dry fodder and concentrate feed to lactating buffalo on a DM basis were 4.33, 5.03, and 5.53 kg, respectively. Similar findings have been reported by Paul et al., (2019) for lactating buffaloes throughout the Haryana.

Methane emission A significant anthropogenic source of CH4 emissions from the agricultural industry is livestock. Enteric fermentation, anaerobic breakdown of organic ele- ments in animal manure, manure deposition on fields and pastures, controlling of dry manure, and liquid manure management methods, like lagoons and holding tanks all contribute to CH4 emissions from livestock production. About 37% of anthropogenic CH4 worldwide came from livestock, which primarily came from ruminants' regular digestive processes, including enteric fermentation (FAO 2006). Methane emission factors from Indian livestock have been revised from time to time. In this research, the total estimated CH4 emission was 127.79 kg CH4 head year for lactating buffalo reared under smallholder village-level farms (Table 4). In this study, the contribution of enteric emission and manure management toward total methane emission was 91.64% and 8.3%, respectively. Results were in line with Chhabra et al., (2009), whose study stated that enteric fermentation contributed 91.3% of total CH4 emis- sions than only 8.7% by manure management. Similarly,

Methane from enteric fermentation Indian livestock accounted for about 12.8-14.7% of the world's enteric CH4 emission considering global enteric "CH4 emission" of 2071 Tg COeq y (FAO 2014). The study predicted the enteric CH4 emission factor of 117.11 kg per head per year while the feed digestibility was estimated at 55.5%. The esti- mated values of emission factors showed a significant range of variation. Multiple studies have been conducted in India.
Table 4 CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation and manure system (Kg/animal/yr)
Sr. No. Methane emission|Lactating buffalo (Mean SD) 1 Average enteric methane emission factor|117.11 +13.08 2 Average manure methane emission factor|10.68 + 0.67 Total|127.79
Springer

Their findings ranged from 50+17 kg. CH4 head year (NATCOM 2004) to 85 kg. CH4 head year (IPCC 2019) for Indian dairy buffalo. The estimates of this study are way high than others findings. Lower feed digestibility of Indian livestock and high methane conversion factor are linked to increased CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation, which might explain the broad range of results. Nevertheless, most of these findings relied on the tier-1 method of the IPCC (2006), while this investigation used the tier-2 technique of IPCC (2019) to assess enteric fermentation emission factor.

Methane from manure Dung cakes were created using nearly 50% dung from buffalo farms. The other half was kept as a dry lot in open space and utilized by the farm- ers as fertilizer for the farm crops as per their requirement without any specific storage period. Generally, farmers kept dried dung cakes for about a year, which they then burned at home. In the present analysis, it was determined that esti- mated methane emissions from manure management were 10.68 kg per head per year. This is significantly record- breaking emission than the previous studies. For Indian dairy bovines, manure CH4 emission factors ranged from 0.8 to 3.3 kg head year Gupta et al (2007).

Higher dry matter intake in the research region may be the cause of the discrepancy between the current survey's estimations and those of other analyses for the manure CH4 emission factor. Rather than the 8% ash content of dung as utilized by the IPCC, the ash content of dung for this study was calculated to be 17%, which resulted in a different emis- sion factor for manure emission. This in turn was associated with greater volatile solid excretion and total energy of con- sumption. Additionally, the study used the most recent IPCC (2019) guidelines, which have caused the difference. Manure can decompose anaerobically and provide a sizable amount of CH4 when it is handled or kept as a liquid (such as in lagoons, ponds, tanks, or pits). The amount of methane gen- erated depends heavily on the storage unit's temperature and retention period. In the research area farmer stored manure prior using to farms for 1-2 year. Manure decomposes more aerobically and produces less CH4 when it is treated as a solid (such as in stacks or piles) or applied to pastures and rangelands. On the same dairy farm, significant seasonal variations in methane emissions from manure management were found. In India, livestock are mostly fed on By-prod- ucts from the agro-industrial sector, including paddy straw, stovers, and wheat straw. These wastes are less VS (Volatile Solids) and more ash as compared to the typical high-quality feed that is provided to livestock in developed countries. All the above listed factors led to the higher methane emission from manure.
Nitrous oxide emission The nitrification and denitrification processes in soils result in the production of nitrous oxide.

It is emitted from manure management and soil manage- ment practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic mat- ter decomposition in the soil. For the current investigation, the total N2O emission was 2.03 kg head year Simi- lar, results were revealed in a study conducted by Baldini et al., (2018) in North Italy, which estimated an emission of 2.12 kg. per head per year from dairy animals.

Nitrous oxide from manure management The treatment and storage of manure before it is put to land or used in any other way, such as for fuel or building, results in the direct and indirect production of N2O in manure manage- ment procedures. The farmers typically held half of the dung in an open confinement area without much vegeta- tion where accumulated manure could be collected peri- odically for about a year, while the other half was sun-dried before being turned into dung cakes and burnt for fuel. In this study, 0.99 kg N2O head-¹ year emission was esti- mated for the present management practices and tropical wet climatic zone. In divergence from the estimates of pre- sent results, Pathak H (2015) reported 0.08 kg per head per year emission from manure management for Indian farms. The estimated values of this research were 12 times higher than the value reported by other researchers. Reasons for the results being on higher side could be attributed to the use of more refined IPCC guidelines and primary data usage for the current investigation which shows a very narrow range of uncertainty associated with the results. Also, the agro-cli- matic conditions of the study area and prolong manure stor- age period, and manure storage system played crucial roles in emissions. The direct N2O ("Nitrous Oxide") was pro- jected to be greater (81.9%) as compared to indirect emis- sion in gaseous and leaching form {N20G (15.8%) and N2OL (2.2%)} of total N2O emissions from manure management (Table 5). The nitrogen excretion, typical animal mass and manure management system being practised by the farmers were positively correlated with nitrous oxide production.
Nitrous oxide from soil management Among the most sig- nificant sources of GHG emissions from milk production is fodder production. Nitrous oxide is (direct + indirect) emit-

ted from soil management practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic matter decomposition in the soil. N2O emis- sions from fodder cultivation for buffalo farms were calcu- lated on the basis of information gathered on green fodder inputs for buffalo, land area, cultivated crops, and yearly output of every crop biomass.
The major fodder for dairy buffalo consisted of oat, sor- ghum, barseem, and maize in the study area. The direct N2O emission has been anticipated as higher (85.3%)

as compared to indirect nitrous oxide {N2OG (2.3%) and N2OL (12.3%)} emissions. Total N2O emissions from soil management due to fodder cultivation for buffalo was higher for oat sorghum followed by barseem, oat, and maize as summarised in Table 6. The farmers in the study area used huge amount of fertilizers for the mentioned fodder crops, leading to high N2O emissions. Urea (46% N) and Diammonium phosphate (18% N) were the main nitrogen sourced fertilizers used at the rate of 264 and 123.51 kg/Hectare, respectively.

The total GHG emission from Lactating buffalo is sum- marised in Table 7. The off-farm concentrate and fodder were the major GHG emissions source, followed by enteric fermentation and manure management.
2: <header>Greenhouse gas emission </header>

Among the most vital elements affecting the GHG emissions from smallholder farm production was the diet of the buf- falo. Table 3 summarizes the quantity of different feed and fodders fed to animals on a Dry Matter (DM) basis in a day.
Table 3 Estimated average dry matter Intake of buffalo

Sr. No.|Category of feed|Ingredients of feed|Kg. day-1 (Mean + SD) 1|Concentrate|Wheat bran|1.61 + 1.2 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed|0.84 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Wheat flour|0.22 + 0.3 1|Concentrate|Pearl millet grain|0.23 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Gram hull|0.31 + 0.6 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed cake|2.32 + 0.7 2|Dry fodder|Wheat straw|4.72 + 1.6 2|Dry fodder|Paddy straw|0.31 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Maize|0.20 + 0.6 3|Green fodder|Local grass|0.77 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Oat|0.42 + 0.5 3|Green fodder|Sorghum|2.32 + 1.1 3|Green fodder|Barseem|0.62 + 0.3 Total|||14.89

The data about feeds and fodders were converted into DM for each feed ingredient by applying DM weight issues sug- gested by Ranjhan, (1991) and Banerjee, (2012) for Indian feed and fodder. The animals in the stalls were only pro- vided with concentrates, agricultural byproducts, and a small amount of green grass to eat. The animals received varying amounts of green forage throughout the seasons. The main consumers of the more expensive feed, such as concentrates, were lactating buffalo. The average green fodder, dry fodder and concentrate feed to lactating buffalo on a DM basis were 4.33, 5.03, and 5.53 kg, respectively. Similar findings have been reported by Paul et al., (2019) for lactating buffaloes throughout the Haryana.

Among the most vital elements affecting the GHG emissions from smallholder farm production was the diet of the buf- falo. Table 3 summarizes the quantity of different feed and fodders fed to animals on a Dry Matter (DM) basis in a day.
Table 3 Estimated average dry matter Intake of buffalo

Sr. No.|Category of feed|Ingredients of feed|Kg. day-1 (Mean + SD) 1|Concentrate|Wheat bran|1.61 + 1.2 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed|0.84 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Wheat flour|0.22 + 0.3 1|Concentrate|Pearl millet grain|0.23 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Gram hull|0.31 + 0.6 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed cake|2.32 + 0.7 2|Dry fodder|Wheat straw|4.72 + 1.6 2|Dry fodder|Paddy straw|0.31 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Maize|0.20 + 0.6 3|Green fodder|Local grass|0.77 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Oat|0.42 + 0.5 3|Green fodder|Sorghum|2.32 + 1.1 3|Green fodder|Barseem|0.62 + 0.3 Total|||14.89

The data about feeds and fodders were converted into DM for each feed ingredient by applying DM weight issues sug- gested by Ranjhan, (1991) and Banerjee, (2012) for Indian feed and fodder. The animals in the stalls were only pro- vided with concentrates, agricultural byproducts, and a small amount of green grass to eat. The animals received varying amounts of green forage throughout the seasons. The main consumers of the more expensive feed, such as concentrates, were lactating buffalo. The average green fodder, dry fodder and concentrate feed to lactating buffalo on a DM basis were 4.33, 5.03, and 5.53 kg, respectively. Similar findings have been reported by Paul et al., (2019) for lactating buffaloes throughout the Haryana.

Methane emission A significant anthropogenic source of CH4 emissions from the agricultural industry is livestock. Enteric fermentation, anaerobic breakdown of organic ele- ments in animal manure, manure deposition on fields and pastures, controlling of dry manure, and liquid manure management methods, like lagoons and holding tanks all contribute to CH4 emissions from livestock production. About 37% of anthropogenic CH4 worldwide came from livestock, which primarily came from ruminants' regular digestive processes, including enteric fermentation (FAO 2006). Methane emission factors from Indian livestock have been revised from time to time. In this research, the total estimated CH4 emission was 127.79 kg CH4 head year for lactating buffalo reared under smallholder village-level farms (Table 4). In this study, the contribution of enteric emission and manure management toward total methane emission was 91.64% and 8.3%, respectively. Results were in line with Chhabra et al., (2009), whose study stated that enteric fermentation contributed 91.3% of total CH4 emis- sions than only 8.7% by manure management. Similarly,

Methane from enteric fermentation Indian livestock accounted for about 12.8-14.7% of the world's enteric CH4 emission considering global enteric "CH4 emission" of 2071 Tg COeq y (FAO 2014). The study predicted the enteric CH4 emission factor of 117.11 kg per head per year while the feed digestibility was estimated at 55.5%. The esti- mated values of emission factors showed a significant range of variation. Multiple studies have been conducted in India.
Table 4 CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation and manure system (Kg/animal/yr)
Sr. No. Methane emission|Lactating buffalo (Mean SD) 1 Average enteric methane emission factor|117.11 +13.08 2 Average manure methane emission factor|10.68 + 0.67 Total|127.79
Springer

Their findings ranged from 50+17 kg. CH4 head year (NATCOM 2004) to 85 kg. CH4 head year (IPCC 2019) for Indian dairy buffalo. The estimates of this study are way high than others findings. Lower feed digestibility of Indian livestock and high methane conversion factor are linked to increased CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation, which might explain the broad range of results. Nevertheless, most of these findings relied on the tier-1 method of the IPCC (2006), while this investigation used the tier-2 technique of IPCC (2019) to assess enteric fermentation emission factor.

Methane from manure Dung cakes were created using nearly 50% dung from buffalo farms. The other half was kept as a dry lot in open space and utilized by the farm- ers as fertilizer for the farm crops as per their requirement without any specific storage period. Generally, farmers kept dried dung cakes for about a year, which they then burned at home. In the present analysis, it was determined that esti- mated methane emissions from manure management were 10.68 kg per head per year. This is significantly record- breaking emission than the previous studies. For Indian dairy bovines, manure CH4 emission factors ranged from 0.8 to 3.3 kg head year Gupta et al (2007).

Higher dry matter intake in the research region may be the cause of the discrepancy between the current survey's estimations and those of other analyses for the manure CH4 emission factor. Rather than the 8% ash content of dung as utilized by the IPCC, the ash content of dung for this study was calculated to be 17%, which resulted in a different emis- sion factor for manure emission. This in turn was associated with greater volatile solid excretion and total energy of con- sumption. Additionally, the study used the most recent IPCC (2019) guidelines, which have caused the difference. Manure can decompose anaerobically and provide a sizable amount of CH4 when it is handled or kept as a liquid (such as in lagoons, ponds, tanks, or pits). The amount of methane gen- erated depends heavily on the storage unit's temperature and retention period. In the research area farmer stored manure prior using to farms for 1-2 year. Manure decomposes more aerobically and produces less CH4 when it is treated as a solid (such as in stacks or piles) or applied to pastures and rangelands. On the same dairy farm, significant seasonal variations in methane emissions from manure management were found. In India, livestock are mostly fed on By-prod- ucts from the agro-industrial sector, including paddy straw, stovers, and wheat straw. These wastes are less VS (Volatile Solids) and more ash as compared to the typical high-quality feed that is provided to livestock in developed countries. All the above listed factors led to the higher methane emission from manure.
Nitrous oxide emission The nitrification and denitrification processes in soils result in the production of nitrous oxide.

It is emitted from manure management and soil manage- ment practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic mat- ter decomposition in the soil. For the current investigation, the total N2O emission was 2.03 kg head year Simi- lar, results were revealed in a study conducted by Baldini et al., (2018) in North Italy, which estimated an emission of 2.12 kg. per head per year from dairy animals.

Nitrous oxide from manure management The treatment and storage of manure before it is put to land or used in any other way, such as for fuel or building, results in the direct and indirect production of N2O in manure manage- ment procedures. The farmers typically held half of the dung in an open confinement area without much vegeta- tion where accumulated manure could be collected peri- odically for about a year, while the other half was sun-dried before being turned into dung cakes and burnt for fuel. In this study, 0.99 kg N2O head-¹ year emission was esti- mated for the present management practices and tropical wet climatic zone. In divergence from the estimates of pre- sent results, Pathak H (2015) reported 0.08 kg per head per year emission from manure management for Indian farms. The estimated values of this research were 12 times higher than the value reported by other researchers. Reasons for the results being on higher side could be attributed to the use of more refined IPCC guidelines and primary data usage for the current investigation which shows a very narrow range of uncertainty associated with the results. Also, the agro-cli- matic conditions of the study area and prolong manure stor- age period, and manure storage system played crucial roles in emissions. The direct N2O ("Nitrous Oxide") was pro- jected to be greater (81.9%) as compared to indirect emis- sion in gaseous and leaching form {N20G (15.8%) and N2OL (2.2%)} of total N2O emissions from manure management (Table 5). The nitrogen excretion, typical animal mass and manure management system being practised by the farmers were positively correlated with nitrous oxide production.

ted from soil management practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic matter decomposition in the soil. N2O emis- sions from fodder cultivation for buffalo farms were calcu- lated on the basis of information gathered on green fodder inputs for buffalo, land area, cultivated crops, and yearly output of every crop biomass.
The major fodder for dairy buffalo consisted of oat, sor- ghum, barseem, and maize in the study area. The direct N2O emission has been anticipated as higher (85.3%)

as compared to indirect nitrous oxide {N2OG (2.3%) and N2OL (12.3%)} emissions. Total N2O emissions from soil management due to fodder cultivation for buffalo was higher for oat sorghum followed by barseem, oat, and maize as summarised in Table 6. The farmers in the study area used huge amount of fertilizers for the mentioned fodder crops, leading to high N2O emissions. Urea (46% N) and Diammonium phosphate (18% N) were the main nitrogen sourced fertilizers used at the rate of 264 and 123.51 kg/Hectare, respectively.

The total GHG emission from Lactating buffalo is sum- marised in Table 7. The off-farm concentrate and fodder were the major GHG emissions source, followed by enteric fermentation and manure management.

ted from soil management practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic matter decomposition in the soil. N2O emis- sions from fodder cultivation for buffalo farms were calcu- lated on the basis of information gathered on green fodder inputs for buffalo, land area, cultivated crops, and yearly output of every crop biomass.
The major fodder for dairy buffalo consisted of oat, sor- ghum, barseem, and maize in the study area. The direct N2O emission has been anticipated as higher (85.3%)

as compared to indirect nitrous oxide {N2OG (2.3%) and N2OL (12.3%)} emissions. Total N2O emissions from soil management due to fodder cultivation for buffalo was higher for oat sorghum followed by barseem, oat, and maize as summarised in Table 6. The farmers in the study area used huge amount of fertilizers for the mentioned fodder crops, leading to high N2O emissions. Urea (46% N) and Diammonium phosphate (18% N) were the main nitrogen sourced fertilizers used at the rate of 264 and 123.51 kg/Hectare, respectively.

The total GHG emission from Lactating buffalo is sum- marised in Table 7. The off-farm concentrate and fodder were the major GHG emissions source, followed by enteric fermentation and manure management.

Sr. No.|Category of feed|Ingredients of feed|Kg. day-1 (Mean + SD) 1|Concentrate|Wheat bran|1.61 + 1.2 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed|0.84 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Wheat flour|0.22 + 0.3 1|Concentrate|Pearl millet grain|0.23 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Gram hull|0.31 + 0.6 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed cake|2.32 + 0.7 2|Dry fodder|Wheat straw|4.72 + 1.6 2|Dry fodder|Paddy straw|0.31 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Maize|0.20 + 0.6 3|Green fodder|Local grass|0.77 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Oat|0.42 + 0.5 3|Green fodder|Sorghum|2.32 + 1.1 3|Green fodder|Barseem|0.62 + 0.3 Total|||14.89

The data about feeds and fodders were converted into DM for each feed ingredient by applying DM weight issues sug- gested by Ranjhan, (1991) and Banerjee, (2012) for Indian feed and fodder. The animals in the stalls were only pro- vided with concentrates, agricultural byproducts, and a small amount of green grass to eat. The animals received varying amounts of green forage throughout the seasons. The main consumers of the more expensive feed, such as concentrates, were lactating buffalo. The average green fodder, dry fodder and concentrate feed to lactating buffalo on a DM basis were 4.33, 5.03, and 5.53 kg, respectively. Similar findings have been reported by Paul et al., (2019) for lactating buffaloes throughout the Haryana.

Methane emission A significant anthropogenic source of CH4 emissions from the agricultural industry is livestock. Enteric fermentation, anaerobic breakdown of organic ele- ments in animal manure, manure deposition on fields and pastures, controlling of dry manure, and liquid manure management methods, like lagoons and holding tanks all contribute to CH4 emissions from livestock production. About 37% of anthropogenic CH4 worldwide came from livestock, which primarily came from ruminants' regular digestive processes, including enteric fermentation (FAO 2006). Methane emission factors from Indian livestock have been revised from time to time. In this research, the total estimated CH4 emission was 127.79 kg CH4 head year for lactating buffalo reared under smallholder village-level farms (Table 4). In this study, the contribution of enteric emission and manure management toward total methane emission was 91.64% and 8.3%, respectively. Results were in line with Chhabra et al., (2009), whose study stated that enteric fermentation contributed 91.3% of total CH4 emis- sions than only 8.7% by manure management. Similarly,

Methane from enteric fermentation Indian livestock accounted for about 12.8-14.7% of the world's enteric CH4 emission considering global enteric "CH4 emission" of 2071 Tg COeq y (FAO 2014). The study predicted the enteric CH4 emission factor of 117.11 kg per head per year while the feed digestibility was estimated at 55.5%. The esti- mated values of emission factors showed a significant range of variation. Multiple studies have been conducted in India.
Table 4 CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation and manure system (Kg/animal/yr)
Sr. No. Methane emission|Lactating buffalo (Mean SD) 1 Average enteric methane emission factor|117.11 +13.08 2 Average manure methane emission factor|10.68 + 0.67 Total|127.79
Springer

Their findings ranged from 50+17 kg. CH4 head year (NATCOM 2004) to 85 kg. CH4 head year (IPCC 2019) for Indian dairy buffalo. The estimates of this study are way high than others findings. Lower feed digestibility of Indian livestock and high methane conversion factor are linked to increased CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation, which might explain the broad range of results. Nevertheless, most of these findings relied on the tier-1 method of the IPCC (2006), while this investigation used the tier-2 technique of IPCC (2019) to assess enteric fermentation emission factor.

Methane from manure Dung cakes were created using nearly 50% dung from buffalo farms. The other half was kept as a dry lot in open space and utilized by the farm- ers as fertilizer for the farm crops as per their requirement without any specific storage period. Generally, farmers kept dried dung cakes for about a year, which they then burned at home. In the present analysis, it was determined that esti- mated methane emissions from manure management were 10.68 kg per head per year. This is significantly record- breaking emission than the previous studies. For Indian dairy bovines, manure CH4 emission factors ranged from 0.8 to 3.3 kg head year Gupta et al (2007).

Higher dry matter intake in the research region may be the cause of the discrepancy between the current survey's estimations and those of other analyses for the manure CH4 emission factor. Rather than the 8% ash content of dung as utilized by the IPCC, the ash content of dung for this study was calculated to be 17%, which resulted in a different emis- sion factor for manure emission. This in turn was associated with greater volatile solid excretion and total energy of con- sumption. Additionally, the study used the most recent IPCC (2019) guidelines, which have caused the difference. Manure can decompose anaerobically and provide a sizable amount of CH4 when it is handled or kept as a liquid (such as in lagoons, ponds, tanks, or pits). The amount of methane gen- erated depends heavily on the storage unit's temperature and retention period. In the research area farmer stored manure prior using to farms for 1-2 year. Manure decomposes more aerobically and produces less CH4 when it is treated as a solid (such as in stacks or piles) or applied to pastures and rangelands. On the same dairy farm, significant seasonal variations in methane emissions from manure management were found. In India, livestock are mostly fed on By-prod- ucts from the agro-industrial sector, including paddy straw, stovers, and wheat straw. These wastes are less VS (Volatile Solids) and more ash as compared to the typical high-quality feed that is provided to livestock in developed countries. All the above listed factors led to the higher methane emission from manure.
Nitrous oxide emission The nitrification and denitrification processes in soils result in the production of nitrous oxide.

It is emitted from manure management and soil manage- ment practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic mat- ter decomposition in the soil. For the current investigation, the total N2O emission was 2.03 kg head year Simi- lar, results were revealed in a study conducted by Baldini et al., (2018) in North Italy, which estimated an emission of 2.12 kg. per head per year from dairy animals.

Nitrous oxide from manure management The treatment and storage of manure before it is put to land or used in any other way, such as for fuel or building, results in the direct and indirect production of N2O in manure manage- ment procedures. The farmers typically held half of the dung in an open confinement area without much vegeta- tion where accumulated manure could be collected peri- odically for about a year, while the other half was sun-dried before being turned into dung cakes and burnt for fuel. In this study, 0.99 kg N2O head-¹ year emission was esti- mated for the present management practices and tropical wet climatic zone. In divergence from the estimates of pre- sent results, Pathak H (2015) reported 0.08 kg per head per year emission from manure management for Indian farms. The estimated values of this research were 12 times higher than the value reported by other researchers. Reasons for the results being on higher side could be attributed to the use of more refined IPCC guidelines and primary data usage for the current investigation which shows a very narrow range of uncertainty associated with the results. Also, the agro-cli- matic conditions of the study area and prolong manure stor- age period, and manure storage system played crucial roles in emissions. The direct N2O ("Nitrous Oxide") was pro- jected to be greater (81.9%) as compared to indirect emis- sion in gaseous and leaching form {N20G (15.8%) and N2OL (2.2%)} of total N2O emissions from manure management (Table 5). The nitrogen excretion, typical animal mass and manure management system being practised by the farmers were positively correlated with nitrous oxide production.

All the above listed factors led to the higher methane emission from manure.
Nitrous oxide emission The nitrification and denitrification processes in soils result in the production of nitrous oxide.

It is emitted from manure management and soil manage- ment practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic mat- ter decomposition in the soil. For the current investigation, the total N2O emission was 2.03 kg head year Simi- lar, results were revealed in a study conducted by Baldini et al., (2018) in North Italy, which estimated an emission of 2.12 kg. per head per year from dairy animals.

Nitrous oxide from manure management The treatment and storage of manure before it is put to land or used in any other way, such as for fuel or building, results in the direct and indirect production of N2O in manure manage- ment procedures. The farmers typically held half of the dung in an open confinement area without much vegeta- tion where accumulated manure could be collected peri- odically for about a year, while the other half was sun-dried before being turned into dung cakes and burnt for fuel. In this study, 0.99 kg N2O head-¹ year emission was esti- mated for the present management practices and tropical wet climatic zone. In divergence from the estimates of pre- sent results, Pathak H (2015) reported 0.08 kg per head per year emission from manure management for Indian farms. The estimated values of this research were 12 times higher than the value reported by other researchers. Reasons for the results being on higher side could be attributed to the use of more refined IPCC guidelines and primary data usage for the current investigation which shows a very narrow range of uncertainty associated with the results. Also, the agro-cli- matic conditions of the study area and prolong manure stor- age period, and manure storage system played crucial roles in emissions. The direct N2O ("Nitrous Oxide") was pro- jected to be greater (81.9%) as compared to indirect emis- sion in gaseous and leaching form {N20G (15.8%) and N2OL (2.2%)} of total N2O emissions from manure management (Table 5). The nitrogen excretion, typical animal mass and manure management system being practised by the farmers were positively correlated with nitrous oxide production.

ted from soil management practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic matter decomposition in the soil. N2O emis- sions from fodder cultivation for buffalo farms were calcu- lated on the basis of information gathered on green fodder inputs for buffalo, land area, cultivated crops, and yearly output of every crop biomass.
The major fodder for dairy buffalo consisted of oat, sor- ghum, barseem, and maize in the study area. The direct N2O emission has been anticipated as higher (85.3%)

as compared to indirect nitrous oxide {N2OG (2.3%) and N2OL (12.3%)} emissions. Total N2O emissions from soil management due to fodder cultivation for buffalo was higher for oat sorghum followed by barseem, oat, and maize as summarised in Table 6. The farmers in the study area used huge amount of fertilizers for the mentioned fodder crops, leading to high N2O emissions. Urea (46% N) and Diammonium phosphate (18% N) were the main nitrogen sourced fertilizers used at the rate of 264 and 123.51 kg/Hectare, respectively.

The total GHG emission from Lactating buffalo is sum- marised in Table 7. The off-farm concentrate and fodder were the major GHG emissions source, followed by enteric fermentation and manure management.

Among the most vital elements affecting the GHG emissions from smallholder farm production was the diet of the buf- falo. Table 3 summarizes the quantity of different feed and fodders fed to animals on a Dry Matter (DM) basis in a day.
Table 3 Estimated average dry matter Intake of buffalo

Sr. No.|Category of feed|Ingredients of feed|Kg. day-1 (Mean + SD) 1|Concentrate|Wheat bran|1.61 + 1.2 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed|0.84 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Wheat flour|0.22 + 0.3 1|Concentrate|Pearl millet grain|0.23 + 0.4 1|Concentrate|Gram hull|0.31 + 0.6 1|Concentrate|Cotton seed cake|2.32 + 0.7 2|Dry fodder|Wheat straw|4.72 + 1.6 2|Dry fodder|Paddy straw|0.31 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Maize|0.20 + 0.6 3|Green fodder|Local grass|0.77 + 0.3 3|Green fodder|Oat|0.42 + 0.5 3|Green fodder|Sorghum|2.32 + 1.1 3|Green fodder|Barseem|0.62 + 0.3 Total|||14.89

The data about feeds and fodders were converted into DM for each feed ingredient by applying DM weight issues sug- gested by Ranjhan, (1991) and Banerjee, (2012) for Indian feed and fodder. The animals in the stalls were only pro- vided with concentrates, agricultural byproducts, and a small amount of green grass to eat. The animals received varying amounts of green forage throughout the seasons. The main consumers of the more expensive feed, such as concentrates, were lactating buffalo. The average green fodder, dry fodder and concentrate feed to lactating buffalo on a DM basis were 4.33, 5.03, and 5.53 kg, respectively. Similar findings have been reported by Paul et al., (2019) for lactating buffaloes throughout the Haryana.

Methane emission A significant anthropogenic source of CH4 emissions from the agricultural industry is livestock. Enteric fermentation, anaerobic breakdown of organic ele- ments in animal manure, manure deposition on fields and pastures, controlling of dry manure, and liquid manure management methods, like lagoons and holding tanks all contribute to CH4 emissions from livestock production. About 37% of anthropogenic CH4 worldwide came from livestock, which primarily came from ruminants' regular digestive processes, including enteric fermentation (FAO 2006). Methane emission factors from Indian livestock have been revised from time to time. In this research, the total estimated CH4 emission was 127.79 kg CH4 head year for lactating buffalo reared under smallholder village-level farms (Table 4). In this study, the contribution of enteric emission and manure management toward total methane emission was 91.64% and 8.3%, respectively. Results were in line with Chhabra et al., (2009), whose study stated that enteric fermentation contributed 91.3% of total CH4 emis- sions than only 8.7% by manure management. Similarly,

Methane from enteric fermentation Indian livestock accounted for about 12.8-14.7% of the world's enteric CH4 emission considering global enteric "CH4 emission" of 2071 Tg COeq y (FAO 2014). The study predicted the enteric CH4 emission factor of 117.11 kg per head per year while the feed digestibility was estimated at 55.5%. The esti- mated values of emission factors showed a significant range of variation. Multiple studies have been conducted in India.
Table 4 CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation and manure system (Kg/animal/yr)
Sr. No. Methane emission|Lactating buffalo (Mean SD) 1 Average enteric methane emission factor|117.11 +13.08 2 Average manure methane emission factor|10.68 + 0.67 Total|127.79
Springer

Their findings ranged from 50+17 kg. CH4 head year (NATCOM 2004) to 85 kg. CH4 head year (IPCC 2019) for Indian dairy buffalo. The estimates of this study are way high than others findings. Lower feed digestibility of Indian livestock and high methane conversion factor are linked to increased CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation, which might explain the broad range of results. Nevertheless, most of these findings relied on the tier-1 method of the IPCC (2006), while this investigation used the tier-2 technique of IPCC (2019) to assess enteric fermentation emission factor.

Methane from manure Dung cakes were created using nearly 50% dung from buffalo farms. The other half was kept as a dry lot in open space and utilized by the farm- ers as fertilizer for the farm crops as per their requirement without any specific storage period. Generally, farmers kept dried dung cakes for about a year, which they then burned at home. In the present analysis, it was determined that esti- mated methane emissions from manure management were 10.68 kg per head per year. This is significantly record- breaking emission than the previous studies. For Indian dairy bovines, manure CH4 emission factors ranged from 0.8 to 3.3 kg head year Gupta et al (2007).

Higher dry matter intake in the research region may be the cause of the discrepancy between the current survey's estimations and those of other analyses for the manure CH4 emission factor. Rather than the 8% ash content of dung as utilized by the IPCC, the ash content of dung for this study was calculated to be 17%, which resulted in a different emis- sion factor for manure emission. This in turn was associated with greater volatile solid excretion and total energy of con- sumption. Additionally, the study used the most recent IPCC (2019) guidelines, which have caused the difference. Manure can decompose anaerobically and provide a sizable amount of CH4 when it is handled or kept as a liquid (such as in lagoons, ponds, tanks, or pits). The amount of methane gen- erated depends heavily on the storage unit's temperature and retention period. In the research area farmer stored manure prior using to farms for 1-2 year. Manure decomposes more aerobically and produces less CH4 when it is treated as a solid (such as in stacks or piles) or applied to pastures and rangelands. On the same dairy farm, significant seasonal variations in methane emissions from manure management were found. In India, livestock are mostly fed on By-prod- ucts from the agro-industrial sector, including paddy straw, stovers, and wheat straw. These wastes are less VS (Volatile Solids) and more ash as compared to the typical high-quality feed that is provided to livestock in developed countries. All the above listed factors led to the higher methane emission from manure.

Springer

Their findings ranged from 50+17 kg. CH4 head year (NATCOM 2004) to 85 kg. CH4 head year (IPCC 2019) for Indian dairy buffalo. The estimates of this study are way high than others findings. Lower feed digestibility of Indian livestock and high methane conversion factor are linked to increased CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation, which might explain the broad range of results. Nevertheless, most of these findings relied on the tier-1 method of the IPCC (2006), while this investigation used the tier-2 technique of IPCC (2019) to assess enteric fermentation emission factor.

Methane from manure Dung cakes were created using nearly 50% dung from buffalo farms. The other half was kept as a dry lot in open space and utilized by the farm- ers as fertilizer for the farm crops as per their requirement without any specific storage period. Generally, farmers kept dried dung cakes for about a year, which they then burned at home. In the present analysis, it was determined that esti- mated methane emissions from manure management were 10.68 kg per head per year. This is significantly record- breaking emission than the previous studies. For Indian dairy bovines, manure CH4 emission factors ranged from 0.8 to 3.3 kg head year Gupta et al (2007).

Higher dry matter intake in the research region may be the cause of the discrepancy between the current survey's estimations and those of other analyses for the manure CH4 emission factor. Rather than the 8% ash content of dung as utilized by the IPCC, the ash content of dung for this study was calculated to be 17%, which resulted in a different emis- sion factor for manure emission. This in turn was associated with greater volatile solid excretion and total energy of con- sumption. Additionally, the study used the most recent IPCC (2019) guidelines, which have caused the difference. Manure can decompose anaerobically and provide a sizable amount of CH4 when it is handled or kept as a liquid (such as in lagoons, ponds, tanks, or pits). The amount of methane gen- erated depends heavily on the storage unit's temperature and retention period. In the research area farmer stored manure prior using to farms for 1-2 year. Manure decomposes more aerobically and produces less CH4 when it is treated as a solid (such as in stacks or piles) or applied to pastures and rangelands. On the same dairy farm, significant seasonal variations in methane emissions from manure management were found. In India, livestock are mostly fed on By-prod- ucts from the agro-industrial sector, including paddy straw, stovers, and wheat straw. These wastes are less VS (Volatile Solids) and more ash as compared to the typical high-quality feed that is provided to livestock in developed countries. All the above listed factors led to the higher methane emission from manure.
Nitrous oxide emission The nitrification and denitrification processes in soils result in the production of nitrous oxide.

It is emitted from manure management and soil manage- ment practices by the transformation of nitrates in the soil as a result of synthetic fertilizers, manure, and organic mat- ter decomposition in the soil. For the current investigation, the total N2O emission was 2.03 kg head year Simi- lar, results were revealed in a study conducted by Baldini et al., (2018) in North Italy, which estimated an emission of 2.12 kg. per head per year from dairy animals.

Nitrous oxide from manure management The treatment and storage of manure before it is put to land or used in any other way, such as for fuel or building, results in the direct and indirect production of N2O in manure manage- ment procedures. The farmers typically held half of the dung in an open confinement area without much vegeta- tion where accumulated manure could be collected peri- odically for about a year, while the other half was sun-dried before being turned into dung cakes and burnt for fuel. In this study, 0.99 kg N2O head-¹ year emission was esti- mated for the present management practices and tropical wet climatic zone. In divergence from the estimates of pre- sent results, Pathak H (2015) reported 0.08 kg per head per year emission from manure management for Indian farms. The estimated values of this research were 12 times higher than the value reported by other researchers. Reasons for the results being on higher side could be attributed to the use of more refined IPCC guidelines and primary data usage for the current investigation which shows a very narrow range of uncertainty associated with the results. Also, the agro-cli- matic conditions of the study area and prolong manure stor- age period, and manure storage system played crucial roles in emissions. The direct N2O ("Nitrous Oxide") was pro- jected to be greater (81.9%) as compared to indirect emis- sion in gaseous and leaching form {N20G (15.8%) and N2OL (2.2%)} of total N2O emissions from manure management (Table 5). The nitrogen excretion, typical animal mass and manure management system being practised by the farmers were positively correlated with nitrous oxide production.

N2O emis- sions from fodder cultivation for buffalo farms were calcu- lated on the basis of information gathered on green fodder inputs for buffalo, land area, cultivated crops, and yearly output of every crop biomass.
The major fodder for dairy buffalo consisted of oat, sor- ghum, barseem, and maize in the study area. The direct N2O emission has been anticipated as higher (85.3%)

as compared to indirect nitrous oxide {N2OG (2.3%) and N2OL (12.3%)} emissions. Total N2O emissions from soil management due to fodder cultivation for buffalo was higher for oat sorghum followed by barseem, oat, and maize as summarised in Table 6. The farmers in the study area used huge amount of fertilizers for the mentioned fodder crops, leading to high N2O emissions. Urea (46% N) and Diammonium phosphate (18% N) were the main nitrogen sourced fertilizers used at the rate of 264 and 123.51 kg/Hectare, respectively.

The total GHG emission from Lactating buffalo is sum- marised in Table 7. The off-farm concentrate and fodder were the major GHG emissions source, followed by enteric fermentation and manure management.
13: The weighted mean CF of the milk produced by 120 lactat- ing buffalo raised on smallholder farms in Haryana from cradle to the farm gate was 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM. The largest GHG emissions directly from buffalo in this study were a result of enteric fermentation (90%), followed by CH4 from manure management (8%), N20 from soil management (0.79%), and N2O from manure management (0.76%). In prior research for lactating cattle in the same region, enteric fermentation (82%) was shown to be the main source of direct greenhouse gas emissions, followed by CH4 from manure management (16%) and N2O from manure man- agement (2%) (Kumar et al. 2023). The animal species and productivity of the dairy animals could be the reason for slight variations in the findings.
