manure management (5.38%), as revealed in Fig. 5. Gerber et al. (2013) observed comparable figures, with 59.5% of entire GHG emissions in the buffalo milk production system coming from enteric fermentation, 17.6% from feed and crop production, and 6% from manure management. According to Fig. 4, there was substantial variation in the CF of milk across the 14 smallholder buffalo farms, ranging between 1.4 and 4.1 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM. According to the IPCC's 100-year characterizations, the mean CF for lactating buffalo was calculated for the cur- rent experiment at 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM. The contri- butions of sources for net CF to produce 1 kg FPCM sum- marised in Table 9. CF of milk production in smallholder dairy systems has received less attention in the scientific literature. Pordhiya and Gautam (2023) in their study at smallholder buffalo farms reported the emission of 3.54 kg CO2 -eq./L of milk. Earlier, it was deemed that livestock farming is always fruitful. But with the growth in knowledge about harmful consequences, newer dimensions will have to be accounted for in the livestock development strategies. Paradoxically, on one side we may have to look for the promotion of smallholder mixed farming systems (generally with low productivity levels) to secure the livelihoods of poor peo- ple, while on the other hand, highly efficient production systems will be required which emit less GHG, use less water and are environment friendly. The dilemma will be to promote two characteristically different production systems in the face of ever-rising demand for livestock produce. The numerous uses of Buffalo were also the primary jus- tifications for preserving the animals from the perspective of a smallholder farmer. Contrary to mixed systems, the pri- mary purpose of maintaining dairy animals in specialized systems is the production of milk. Due to the distinct nature of the two systems, they must not be analyzed in the same manner, i.e., LCA production units of specialized systems is not suitable for "multi-functional mixed" systems. Being

an industrial instrument, the LCA struggles to understand the multi-functionality component when employed in a smallholder dairy system. Additionally, ISO 14044 (2006) advised that system comparisons be based on the same func- tions, measured with the same functional unit. Therefore, if the output of the systems varies, a direct comparison of milk CF from dairy systems with varying levels of specialization is inappropriate. Therefore, the multi-functionality feature should be seriously taken into account in future LCAs on smallholder systems.

6: <header>Transportation </header>

Fuel use for transportation is another CO2 emission source in the dairy production system. Three sub-stages make up the product transportation process: the transportation of raw milk, packaging materials, and cleaning agents. To calculate the quantity of gasoline used during transportation. Equation (3) was used to determine the total amount of energy and CO2 production during the transportation operations involved in producing milk.

7: <header>Processing emissions </header>

The product processing factories reported their overall elec- trical and fuel energy usage. According to this study, the energy source used for processing milk in terms of GHG emissions was electricity, rice husk and coal. All electric- ity used at the plant, with the exception of that designated for container formation, was used during the processing phase. This included all overhead consumption for things like lighting, office equipment, compressed air, refrigeration, and HVAC (Heating, Ventilating, and Air Conditioning). Figure 7 (A), (B), and (C) show the contribution of energy sources in processing-related emissions during the calendar year of 2022-23 for Ghee, curd, and Milk pasteurization, respectively.
8: <header>Packaging emission </header>

The manufacture of LDPE has the overall lowest GWP environmental effect of all the milk packaging materials, whereas the production of HDPE has greater GWP envi- ronmental consequences. It should be noted, though, that this effect results from non-recycling, and that it may be lessened if the aluminium and plastic layers were recycled. The kind of packaging, the quantity of packaging per unit of the product, and the package design can all have an impact on the CF. Given that it is enough to preserve the product's quality, less packaging per product inevitably led to a lower CF. Despite the fact that packaging made up a far less por- tion of the CF than the dairy product itself, package design was a key factor in lowering product losses.

10: <header>CF of dairy processing </header>

The CFs related to the 4 phases for the three products are listed in Table 12. In the present study processing of milk at the dairy plant contributed the least, around 0.24 kg CO2-eq to the CF of FU for the system boundary farm gate to the factory gate. Results of this study hold consistency with the

findings of Zhao et el., (2017), his study included dairy pro- cessing, packaging waste disposal, and milk product trans- portation at a Chinese branded milk plant, revealing the CF of 0.27 kg CO2-eq/Lt. Considerable differences could be attributed to the selection of retail and consumer activities in the system boundary of his study. Figure 8 summarises the relative contribution of different emission processes towards GWP1/ 100

In this study, the highest CF is of ghee (clarified butter) production which is 4.2 kg CO2-eq. Yan and Holden (2018) conducted a study at an Irish dairy plant and reported the CF value of butter from 0.40- 0.62 kg CO2-eq/Kg. Curd production has CF of 1.8 kg CO2-eq. Results of the current research were in line with the results of Mesquita and Car- valho (2023), in his study he found CF of 1.9 kg CO2-eq/ Kg yogurt.
High emission for the study can be assigned to the usage of coal and rice husk ('80%) as an energy source than elec- tricity (12%), the latter shared a low emission factor as

compared to coal and rice husk. This study raised an objec- tion to butter manufacturing, as it revealed it is not an envi- ronment-friendly and sustainable process. 13: The term CF referred to the total amount of carbon diox- ide emitted by a given good or service over the course of its entire life cycle, whether those emissions were caused directly and/or indirectly by the agents (groups, persons, or departments) involved in a given activity. According to Kulak et al. (2016), the goal of LCA was to assist businesses and organizations in evaluating the environmental influence of a product's entire supply chain, identify the sector with the highest emissions, and then suggest efficient strategies for reducing emissions while also maximizing resource dis- tribution and utilization.

In this investigation, the overall CF for the produc- tion of unit Kg of ghee, curd, and pasteurized milk for the impact category being GWP 100 were 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq/ Kg of product, respectively. Comparing our study's findings for the packaging of ghee, curd, and milk from cra- dle to factory gate with studies that examined the same prod- ucts, various disparities between them can be seen in Figs. 9, 10, and 11. These variations came from calculation tech- niques (such as characterization factors), conversion factors (such as densities), inventory data sources, milk production regimes, allocation strategies, and energy sources. It should be emphasized, nonetheless, that despite these variations, the results of our investigation were within the range of values examined for GWP. As a result, the findings of the analysis

Fig. 9 Global Warming Potential (GWP) impact of Ghee (clarified butter): a this study; b Flysjö, A. M. (2012); c Mesquita and Carvalho (2023); d Bruno et al., (2019); e Vergé et al., (2013); f Mahath and Dubey (2019); g Frey and Bruckner (2021)

Fig. 10 Global warming potential (GWP) impact of curd/ yoghurt: a this study; b Flysjö, A. M. (2012); c Mesquita and Carvalho (2023); d Bruno et al., (2019); e Vergé et al., (2013); f Mahath and Dubey (2019); g Vasilaki et al., (2016)
may serve as benchmark values for further research on the dairy industry in India.

The CF of dairy products is primarily influenced by GHG emissions from the farm-level production of raw milk. At the basic production stage, the two main sources of emissions, CH4 (from manure management and enteric fermentation) and N20 (from the manufacture and application of fertilizer) accounted for nearly 70% of the total GHG emissions. The CF of dairy products was primarily determined by the quan- tity of raw milk needed, i.e., the greater the quantity of raw milk required for a product, the greater its CF. The variation in the CF of raw milk among somewhat comparable dairy farms showed that there is room to cut GHG emissions by improving management techniques. The uniqueness of farms must be considered in mitigation plans. Common mitiga- tion strategies concentrate on raising livestock production by feeding diets that are nutritionally balanced and improving

Fig. 11 Global warming potential (GWP) impact of Pasturized milk: a this study; b Flysjö, A. M. (2012); c Mesquita and Carvalho (2023); d Bruno et al., (2019); e Vergé et al., (2013); f Frey and Bruckner (2021); g Zhao et al., (2017)

waste management. From the perspective of a single prod- uct, such as milk, retaining fewer high-yielding animals and substituting manure for synthetic fertilizers may increase milk output while lowering the CF of milk. Dropping the number of animals does not make sense when taking into account the smallholder system's multifunctionality, since all animals serve significant functions and provide the farmer with financial benefits.

There were certain important factors to take into account in the post-farm dairy chain in addition to the difficulties with pro- ducing raw milk. When it comes to reducing GHG emissions for dairy products, there is no "silver bullet"; rather, a number of seemingly insignificant changes over time can add up to consid- erable reductions. Small farm-level changes can lead to rather significant drops in the CF of dairy products (compared to other life cycle stages). Considering that raw milk contributes the most to the CF of dairy products, it is crucial to reduce product losses along the whole value chain, from cradle to factory gate. Setting system limits is a crucial component as many studies only cover a portion of the full daily life cycle. The section before spoke about certain enhancement prospects, but the LCA perspective was not used to confirm them. More study is required, particu- larly on customer behaviour and strategies for reducing product losses. Another crucial element is the latter, which may also be recorded in the FU. The FU is frequently overly simplistically reported and requires more methodological refinement, such as accounting for dietary nutrients. As the intermediary between farmers and customers, dairy firms have a vital role to play in encouraging sustainable agricultural methods and more sustain- able consumption. Even if a company's primary goal is to turn a profit, maintaining a strong commitment to the advancement of environmentally friendly production and consumption (such as the mitigation of climate change) is crucial for its image and profitability in the long run.

Fig. 10 Global warming potential (GWP) impact of curd/ yoghurt: a this study; b Flysjö, A. M. (2012); c Mesquita and Carvalho (2023); d Bruno et al., (2019); e Vergé et al., (2013); f Mahath and Dubey (2019); g Vasilaki et al., (2016)
may serve as benchmark values for further research on the dairy industry in India.

The CF of dairy products is primarily influenced by GHG emissions from the farm-level production of raw milk. At the basic production stage, the two main sources of emissions, CH4 (from manure management and enteric fermentation) and N20 (from the manufacture and application of fertilizer) accounted for nearly 70% of the total GHG emissions. The CF of dairy products was primarily determined by the quan- tity of raw milk needed, i.e., the greater the quantity of raw milk required for a product, the greater its CF. The variation in the CF of raw milk among somewhat comparable dairy farms showed that there is room to cut GHG emissions by improving management techniques. The uniqueness of farms must be considered in mitigation plans. Common mitiga- tion strategies concentrate on raising livestock production by feeding diets that are nutritionally balanced and improving

Fig. 11 Global warming potential (GWP) impact of Pasturized milk: a this study; b Flysjö, A. M. (2012); c Mesquita and Carvalho (2023); d Bruno et al., (2019); e Vergé et al., (2013); f Frey and Bruckner (2021); g Zhao et al., (2017)

waste management. From the perspective of a single prod- uct, such as milk, retaining fewer high-yielding animals and substituting manure for synthetic fertilizers may increase milk output while lowering the CF of milk. Dropping the number of animals does not make sense when taking into account the smallholder system's multifunctionality, since all animals serve significant functions and provide the farmer with financial benefits.

There were certain important factors to take into account in the post-farm dairy chain in addition to the difficulties with pro- ducing raw milk. When it comes to reducing GHG emissions for dairy products, there is no "silver bullet"; rather, a number of seemingly insignificant changes over time can add up to consid- erable reductions. Small farm-level changes can lead to rather significant drops in the CF of dairy products (compared to other life cycle stages). Considering that raw milk contributes the most to the CF of dairy products, it is crucial to reduce product losses along the whole value chain, from cradle to factory gate. Setting system limits is a crucial component as many studies only cover a portion of the full daily life cycle. The section before spoke about certain enhancement prospects, but the LCA perspective was not used to confirm them. More study is required, particu- larly on customer behaviour and strategies for reducing product losses. Another crucial element is the latter, which may also be recorded in the FU. The FU is frequently overly simplistically reported and requires more methodological refinement, such as accounting for dietary nutrients. As the intermediary between farmers and customers, dairy firms have a vital role to play in encouraging sustainable agricultural methods and more sustain- able consumption. Even if a company's primary goal is to turn a profit, maintaining a strong commitment to the advancement of environmentally friendly production and consumption (such as the mitigation of climate change) is crucial for its image and profitability in the long run.
18: The term CF referred to the total amount of carbon diox- ide emitted by a given good or service over the course of its entire life cycle, whether those emissions were caused directly and/or indirectly by the agents (groups, persons, or departments) involved in a given activity. According to Kulak et al. (2016), the goal of LCA was to assist businesses and organizations in evaluating the environmental influence of a product's entire supply chain, identify the sector with the highest emissions, and then suggest efficient strategies for reducing emissions while also maximizing resource dis- tribution and utilization.

In this investigation, the overall CF for the produc- tion of unit Kg of ghee, curd, and pasteurized milk for the impact category being GWP 100 were 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq/ Kg of product, respectively. Comparing our study's findings for the packaging of ghee, curd, and milk from cra- dle to factory gate with studies that examined the same prod- ucts, various disparities between them can be seen in Figs. 9, 10, and 11. These variations came from calculation tech- niques (such as characterization factors), conversion factors (such as densities), inventory data sources, milk production regimes, allocation strategies, and energy sources. It should be emphasized, nonetheless, that despite these variations, the results of our investigation were within the range of values examined for GWP. As a result, the findings of the analysis

may serve as benchmark values for further research on the dairy industry in India.

waste management. When it comes to reducing GHG emissions for dairy products, there is no "silver bullet"; rather, a number of seemingly insignificant changes over time can add up to consid- erable reductions. Small farm-level changes can lead to rather significant drops in the CF of dairy products (compared to other life cycle stages). Setting system limits is a crucial component as many studies only cover a portion of the full daily life cycle. The section before spoke about certain enhancement prospects, but the LCA perspective was not used to confirm them. More study is required, particu- larly on customer behaviour and strategies for reducing product losses. Another crucial element is the latter, which may also be recorded in the FU. The FU is frequently overly simplistically reported and requires more methodological refinement, such as accounting for dietary nutrients. 19: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

21: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

22: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

Author contributions Dr. Gautam conceptualized the idea of this research. Data collection and schedule preparation were carried out by Rakesh Kumar. The methodology was written by Ekta Rani. Anika Malik and Rakesh Kumar analyzed, edited and revised the final ver- sion of the paper. The final document was reviewed and approved by all authors.

The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

24: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

25: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

Anika Malik and Rakesh Kumar analyzed, edited and revised the final ver- sion of the paper. The final document was reviewed and approved by all authors.

Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

27: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

28: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

Anika Malik and Rakesh Kumar analyzed, edited and revised the final ver- sion of the paper. The final document was reviewed and approved by all authors.

The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

30: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

31: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

Author contributions Dr. Gautam conceptualized the idea of this research. Data collection and schedule preparation were carried out by Rakesh Kumar. The methodology was written by Ekta Rani. Anika Malik and Rakesh Kumar analyzed, edited and revised the final ver- sion of the paper. The final document was reviewed and approved by all authors.

The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.

33: The study examined the LCA of smallholder buffalo farms and a renowned dairy plant of Haryana, estimated GHG emission from them and finally analyse the carbon footprint of raw milk and three major dairy products consumed in the state. Accord- ing to the findings, enteric CH4, manure CH4 and manure N2O emissions from buffalo production accounted for 70% of the overall GHG emissions from the smallholder farms. The indirect GHG source, which included seed production, off-farm feed, soil management and other factors, contributed 30% to the emission. On an average, 117.11 and 10.68 kg methane emis- sion per buffalo per year was reported from enteric and manure emission, respectively at smallholder farms. The average carbon footprint for raw milk production was estimated to be 2.24 kg CO2-eq/Kg FPCM, which is an alarming value. Buffalo breed- ing programmes and culling of low producing animals can be

taken into account to enhance per unit milk production while emitting less methane. The higher estimates of emissions from manure management (0.992 kg N2O per head per year) can eas- ily be put to limit by reducing the storage period of manure and use of anaerobic digesting chambers. Green fodder crops which were feed to the buffaloes involved in the present study emitted 124.57 kg N2O per year, as mentioned earlier the farmers used more than the required amount of nitrogenous fertilizer per hec- tare. The government should impart free trainings to the farm- ers to help them adopt modern, sustainable livestock practices. Until and unless, the mitigation strategies are convenient and price convincing to the farmers, which also provides an income creation, smallholder farmers will not be interested in adoption of such practises.

Ghee (4.2) had the largest contribution to the GWP effect (Kg CO2-eq Kg FU), followed by curd (1.8) and pasteurization of milk (0.24). Overall, a unit production of ghee, curd and pasteur- ized milk emitted 39.25, 4.28, and 3.6 kg CO2-eq, respectively. The CF of products is mainly influenced by the CF of raw milk production and the requirement of raw milk per kg Ghee, curd and Pasteurized milk is 19.4, 1.1, 1.54 kg, respectively. Despite the dairy processing plant's efficiency, there is room for develop- ment from an environmental point of view. The results describe many possibilities for GHG mitigation. Use of electricity as a source of power and heat generation instead of coal, diesel and rice husk, and the inclusion of energy-efficient machines, and insulate chambers are advised to decrease the heat loss to low down the emission form dairy processing plant. The examined dairy plant's environmental performance can be raised to a standard level by implementing the suggestions. The purpose of the current research is to offer customers accurate data about carbon emissions, to motivate dairy businesses to take steps to reduce emissions and to advance a low-carbon dairy industry. More study is required on the environment of the Indian dairy industry's various production methods (commercial dairy farms, village level farms, etc.), dairy plants, and production locations (Co-operatives, private and unor- ganized dairy channels). We stress the need of evaluating specialized farms with regard to multifunctionality before comparing them to smallholder systems.
